evidence.quote: The importance of sound to the Atlantic cod, Gadus morhua, and the Atlantic haddock, Melanogrammus aeglefinus
Different sounds are produced in different behavioral contexts and are emitted almost continuously by cod and haddock, especially throughout the spawning season
Listening for the cod and haddock sounds provides a reliable, non-invasive technique for detecting their locations, and especially their spawning areas, and enables them to be protected well.

evidence.quote: The aquarium tanks were more than 10 meters in lateral extent and up to 5 meters deep... supplied with water continuously pumped from the North Sea.
The haddock produced a range of sounds in different contexts in the aquarium at the MLA. Both male and female haddock produced short sequences of repeated 'knocks'
Atlantic haddock (Hawkins and Chapman, 1966) and the Atlantic cod (Chapman and Hawkins, 1973)

Balsfjord is a subarctic fjord (90 km2) with a shallow (30m) depth at the opening to its entrance, but with depths in the inner fjord dropping to 190m. We detected the haddock sounds using a hydrophone held beneath a travelling boat.
Long sequences of repeated knocks were heard at a particular deep location in the fjord... At night, the sounds merged into a continuous and loud low-frequency rumble
Casaretto et al., 2014
Spawning haddock were later found at the same unique location in Balsfjord in several successive years.
Casaretto et al., 2014
An acoustic range was set up at a depth of 21m. Underwater sound projectors, capable of generating frequencies down to about 30Hz
Fish hearing thresholds were determined over a range of pure tone frequencies, providing audiograms from several marine fishes, including the cod... and later the haddock.
Hawkins and Chapman (1975); Hawkins and Johnstone, 1978
The cod were tracked using a pulsed ultrasonic transmitter inserted into the stomach... movements of cod in enclosed sea-lochs (Loch Beag and Loch Torridon)... depths between 10 and 20m
moving within restricted home ranges... The majority of Torridon cod were more active during the day rather than the night
